summary: Written just before Apple's September 2017 event, Gruber predicts that the new high-end iPhone X's name should be pronounced 'ex' rather than 'ten.' He lays out a systematic case based on practical naming concerns: people naturally read X as 'ex,' calling it 'ten' would make the iPhone 8 look outdated, and 'ten' creates impossible naming problems for future models. He acknowledges the 10th anniversary connection but argues the Roman numeral reference works implicitly even with an 'ex' pronunciation.
key_points: Apple's own experience with Mac OS X shows people naturally pronounce X as 'ex' regardless of intent
Calling it 'ten' would make the simultaneously-debuting iPhone 8 look two generations behind rather than new and desirable
Pronouncing it 'ten' creates unsolvable naming problems for both its own successor and future numbered iPhones
Pronouncing it 'ex' preserves the 10th anniversary reference implicitly while enabling a sensible product line (X2, X3, etc.)
money_quotes: People see an "X" and they say "ex".
If it's a "ten", that makes the iPhones 8 it is debuting alongside look instantly out of date.
Apple's goal should be to make all three new iPhones look new, exciting, and desirable.
Calling it "iPhone Ten" only makes sense if this is a one-time product, and making a one-time product makes no sense to me.
Racer X was cool as shit.
key_insight: Naming a product with an X invites a pronunciation problem Apple already experienced with Mac OS X, and calling it 'ten' would create cascading naming problems that 'ex' neatly avoids.
